title: Ozempic rival poised to shake up Dubai weight-loss drug market – EXCLUSIVE - Arabian Business: Latest News
summary: Experts call for patience as approved peptide-based drugs could be 'next big breakthrough'
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
Ozempic and Mounjaro, two of the more notorious weight-loss drugs popular in Dubai, are being rivalled by the rising demand for alternative peptide treatments.

And, despite doctors warning the trend is being pushed faster by social media than by clinical evidence, approved peptide-based drugs have been touted as the “next real breakthrough”.

“Peptides are likely the next wellness trend, but many are overhyped. My view is that peptides are not fake as a category, but the wellness marketing is ahead of the science,” Gehad Masri, UAE-based General surgeon, Proctologist and founder of Yugen Care Clinic said in an exclusive interview with Arabian Business.

“The next real breakthrough will probably come from approved peptide-based drugs, not random ‘research peptides’ from medspas.”

What do drugs like Ozempic do, and what are peptides?

Peptides are short chains of amino acids that act as signalling molecules in the body. Some peptide-based medicines are already well established, including GLP-1 drugs used for diabetes and obesity treatment.

Videos have been circulating online of users comparing themselves before and after peptide treatments citing benefits such as clearer skin, weight-loss, muscle definition and more. The trend has recently been gaining traction on social media.

Drugs such as Ozempic and Mounjaro are peptide-based prescription medicines with strong clinical evidence for diabetes and weight loss. However, experts warned that many peptides now being promoted in wellness clinics for longevity, recovery, metabolism and anti-ageing have less scientific proof and weaker regulatory oversight.

The warning comes as global demand for metabolic and weight-loss treatments continue to rise. According to IQVIA, the global obesity medicines market reached $66 billion in list-processed sales in 2025 and is forecast to rise to $92 billion in 2026 with longer-term estimates ranging from $105 billion to $200 from 2027 and beyond.

“One of the biggest misconceptions around these medications is that they simply cause weight loss. What they are actually doing is altering appetite signalling, slowing gastric emptying and improving glucose regulation. In clinical practice, we often see that weight gain is only a symptom of deeper metabolic dysfunction including insulin resistance, hormonal imbalance and chronic inflammation,” Dr. Areej I. Hilles, Co-Founder and Medical Director of Molodost Longevity Clinic, said.

Other misunderstandings in peptides

The popularity of GLP-1 medicines has also widened consumer interest in other injectable treatments, including peptides marketed for energy, recovery, skin quality, sleep and healthy ageing.

“Peptides are often misunderstood as just a weight-loss shortcut, but their real role is much broader. Some peptides target appetite and metabolism, while others are being studied for recovery, muscle preservation, inflammation, hormone balance, tissue repair, and healthy ageing,” Mr Masri said.

“The problem is that social media often markets all peptides as ‘miracle fat-loss injections,’ when in reality they are very different compounds with very different levels of scientific evidence,” he added.

The peptide trend has gained momentum across wellness and biohacking circles globally. The American Medical Association warned in April that newer injectable peptides can be difficult to assess because candidates, dosing, safety and sourcing are less clear than with regulated GLP-1 medications such as semaglutide or tirzepatide.

What about the UAE?

In the UAE, the trend is being amplified by the country’s broader wellness economy. According to The Global Wellness Institute, the UAE wellness economy is worth $40.8 billion, while the country’s wellness tourism sector is worth $11.3 billion after growing at a compound annual growth rate of 23.5 per cent.

“People are no longer waiting to treat disease. They are investing earlier in prevention, fitness, metabolic health, aesthetics, and healthy aging. Social media and the success of GLP-1 medications also accelerated interest in advanced wellness therapies across the region,” he said.

Previously, the demand for peptide therapy was coming from people focused on weight loss or bodybuilding. However, interest is now coming from professionals, executives, women in midlife, biohackers, fitness-focused adults and patients seeking improvements in longevity, recovery, energy, sleep and metabolic health.

In recent times, patients are looking beyond weight loss alone and asking about muscle preservation, inflammation, hormone balance, recovery and long-term vitality.

“Medications can be a powerful tool, but they work best when combined with a structured medical approach that addresses the underlying drivers of metabolic health. As these therapies become more accessible, the conversation will increasingly shift from weight loss alone to long-term metabolic resilience and disease prevention,” Hilles said.

According to Masri, the risk is that consumers may assume popularity is the same as medical validation.

“The biggest misconception is that all peptides are equally safe, approved, and scientifically proven. Many patients assume that if something is popular online, it must be medically validated,” he said.

“Another common misunderstanding is thinking peptides are “magic injections” that replace nutrition, exercise, sleep, and lifestyle habits. In reality, even the best peptide therapies work best as part of a broader health strategy, not as a shortcut.”

The biggest concern

The most serious concern is unregulated access.

“Many peptides sold online are unregulated research products with uncertain purity, dosing, or safety. Patients may not know what they are actually injecting. Without proper medical guidance, there is also a risk of side effects, contamination, hormonal disruption, or interactions with underlying health conditions.” he said.

The safety debate is not limited to experimental peptides. Even regulated GLP-1 drugs require clinical oversight. A March 2026 study analysing more than 410,000 Reddit posts on semaglutide and tirzepatide found that among users self-reporting use of the drugs, 43.5 per cent described at least one side effect, with gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, fatigue, vomiting, constipation and diarrhoea among the most common.

That makes proper diagnosis, follow-up and medical monitoring critical as wellness consumers increasingly seek injectable therapies.

Masri said red flags include clinics promising “miracle” results, guaranteed weight loss, age reversal or rapid muscle gain without proper evaluation. Other warning signs include no lab testing, no follow-up monitoring, one-size-fits-all peptide packages, heavy social media marketing with limited scientific discussion, unclear sourcing or regulatory status and encouragement of long-term injections without clear medical indication.

“A credible clinic focuses on evidence, safety, realistic expectations, and individualised care, not hype,” he said.

The rise of peptide therapy also raises questions about how longevity is being marketed.

“The risk is that longevity becomes driven by hype instead of science. Patients may spend large amounts of money on unproven treatments, delay proper medical care, or use therapies without understanding the long-term risks,” Masri said.

“True longevity medicine should focus on evidence-based prevention, metabolic health, cardiovascular health, sleep, nutrition, exercise, and careful medical monitoring, not just trendy injections or ‘biohacking’ shortcuts.”

Dubai is expected to remain a major market for personalised wellness and regenerative medicine over the next five years.

Masri said the city is well placed to become a global hub because of its healthcare infrastructure, luxury wellness ecosystem, medical innovation and population interest in healthy ageing, aesthetics and performance.

“Over the next five years, I think Dubai will become one of the global hubs for personalised wellness, preventive medicine, and regenerative therapies,” he said. “Dubai is uniquely positioned because it combines medical innovation, luxury wellness, advanced healthcare infrastructure, and a population highly engaged in performance, aesthetics, and healthy aging.”

But the future of the sector will depend on whether clinics can separate medical potential from marketing hype.

“The most important point is that wellness should remain science-led, not trend-led. Peptides, regenerative medicine, and longevity therapies are exciting fields with real potential, but sustainable health still comes from fundamentals: nutrition, movement, sleep, stress management, and proper medical care,” Masri said.

“The future of medicine is becoming more personalised and preventive, and that is a very positive shift if it is approached responsibly,” he concluded.
